rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Technical support to education sector
rows.description:
The Government of Finland has contributed to development in the education sector since 1999 and is currently one of the development partners supporting the Nepali Government with financing and implementation of the School Sector Reform Plan (SSRP). The ove
rall objective is to contribute to enhancing quality and relevance of general education by introducing TEVT soft skills (vocationally oriented skills) in school system in order to improve employability of students and to meet the demands of labor market. T
he objective has been discussed with all major stakeholders and has been found to be relevant. No changes are required to the overall objective. The purpose of the Technical Assistance is to support the Ministry of Education in implementing the TEVT (Soft 
Skills) component of the SSRP that is operationalized in Annual Strategic Implementation Plans (ASIP) of each year.

rows.title: Frame agreement with NGO (Fida Int.)
rows.description:
Water and Sanitation Project WATSAN. The project purpose is the decreased incidence of intestinal deceases in rural communities in target VDCs in Kailali District, Far West, Nepal. Fida's partner, the New Life Service Association (NSA), will have project a
ctivities like awareness raising and capacity building of communities on health, sanitation and hygiene and water supply and toilet construction. It will use community mobilization and work in close coordination with partner agencies. The project will form
 Health and Sanitation groups (called WASH groups) in each VDC to address the root causes (contaminated water, poor sanitation and personal hygiene practices) of the main problem, high incidence of the intestinal diseases.

Overall objective of the project is to build an environmentally-sustainable value chain for allo from field to district-level centre for the benefit of 39 women allo producing groups in Sankhuwasabha Solukhumbu and Bhojpur Districts of Eastern Nepal.Specif
ic objectives are to ensure sustainable supply of raw material through cultivation and management of allo on private and common lands to adopt appropriate technologies for allo extraction and spinning and to build the capacity of newly registered   cottage
 industry for a sustainable allo enterprise.The main avtivities are: Training materials and co-funding support for allo cultivation Testing procurement and installation of spinning machine set that is adapted for allo fibres, establishing procedures for ef
ficient and equitable ownership and management, training local people on how to operate it Replication of environmentally-sound steaming techniques for fibre extraction to reduce fuel-wood usage and improve productivity Capacity-building of a self-supporti
ng producer-controlled community-based cottage industry to deliver services to producer groups.Expected results of the projec are: Increase in volume of allo raw material supply by 50% as compared with benchmark through environmentally-sustainable cultivat
ion and management of allo on private and common lands. Spinning machine set installed producing consistent quality allo yarn with corresponding reduction of unhealthy use of the drop spindle Energy-efficient extraction of allo fibre using a steaming metho

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Project-Western Nepal Completion Phase
rows.description:
RWSSP Completion Phase will continue support to advance rural water supply and sanitation in western Nepal. Nepal's national goal is to reach total clean water and sanitation coverage by 2017. Access to clean water and sanitation is one of the basic human 
rights. In this respect awareness of rural population on these rights will be increased and the capacity of the 'duty bearers' i.e. national institutions to respond with sufficient service provision will be strengthened. A phasing-out strategy will be prep
ared to ensure transfer of responsibilities and sustainability of results after the end of the project. Expansion of the project's scope to rural livelihoods will be explored during the planning phase. Arsenic mitigation will be taken into account in the T
erai-region.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Improving rehabilitation services for people with spinal cord injury in Western Nepal
rows.description:
Spinal cord injury rehabilitation is a major unmet need in Nepal. In Nepal most cases are caused by falls from heights chiefly from trees (from which poor farmers cut fodder for their stock) and building sites. Road traffic accidents are an increasing caus
e of SCI. There are currently only three centres in the whole country offering rehabilitation services to people with spinal cord injuries ? two of the three are in Kathmandu. GPHRC provides services to the Western Development Region and through four INF c
linics to the Mid-Western Development Region - a total population of around 8.5 million people. At present large proportion of SCI patients present for rehabilitation with pressure sores. These patients require admissions up to 6 months as the pressure sor
e needs to heal before rehabilitation can commence. Patients admitted without pressure sores can complete the rehabilitation within 6-8 weeks. The project ?Improving rehabilitation services for people with spinal cord injury in western Nepal? will be imple
mented by International Nepal Fellowship Nepal?s (INF/Nepal) Green Pastures Hospital and Rehabilitation Centre (GPHRC) in collaboration with government and private hospitals in Pokhara Nepal and INF?s Partnership for Rehabilitation (PFR) which supports com
munity-based rehabilitation services in the Western Region of Nepal. This project has three major objectives:1. To reduce the incidence of secondary complications in new cases of spinal cord injury (SCI) by providing health education and essential equipmen
t to patients in primary (trauma) care 2. To provide better quality rehabilitation services to SCI clients through building a appropriately designed 12 bed SCI ward block at GPHRC 3. To reduce the incidence of infections and pressure sores in SCI patients 
after their return home and thereby reduce the rate of re-admission and improve their quality of life

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Local Cooperation Fund (LCF) in Nepal
rows.description:
The project aims to contribute for creating democratic and credible governance and management system in NGOs/CSOs of Nepal that could be scaled up to Nepali politics and society. The overall objective of the project is to increase access and voices of peop
le to basic services and entitlements as the rights-holders by expanding the roles of NGOs/CSOs in democratic governance and development which ultimately contribute for sustainable peace and conflict transformation in the country. The project aims to devel
op the capacity of NGOs/CSOs for embedding the key elements of institutional good governance which will enhance effectiveness of NGOs/CSOs in development process of Nepal. It will give a greater credibility to them for the development process they facilita
te. The specific objective of the project is to improve governance and management of NGOs/CSOs for effective delivery of basic services in the country by enhancing their legitimacy transparency accountability and integrity.

rows.description:
The overall objective of the project is to  create national movement Teach for Nepal of young people to enhance quality of education in public schools in Nepal.  The project aims to attract outstanding university graduates and young professionals to teach 
in public and community schools for two years as Fellows primarily in maths science and English to decrease achievement gaps that exists between private and public schools students. The project will be co-funded with Nepalese private sector companies. Priv
ate sector will also offer working placements for the Fellows after their two years engagement is public schools.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Mobilizing Youth for Gender Equality in Politics
rows.description:
Discrimination and unequal share of power are considered the major bottleneck for democracy in Nepal. Discrimination has restricted women and members of other marginalized groups from equal access to the state?s policy making positions. The proposed Action
 supports building up the pluralistic and representative democracy in Nepal by supporting cross-party forums for dialogue promote the use of non-violent activities for transformation and increase women's capacity to engage in political processes. The actio
n is based on notion that Women's meaningful participation in political processes reinforces sustainable peace and reduces the risk of politically initiated violence and halt in democratic processes.The Joint Youth and Student Platform in Nepal with suppor
t of DEMO Finland has experience and proven strengths of challenging the status quo of political party organisations as well as together tackle difficult cultural matters in society. This project will strengthen the Platform as a cross-party dialogue centr
e which has the will and the know-how to promote inclusion pluralism and equal participation in political parties.The duration of the project is 29 months in 2013-2015 and the proposed funding for the project is 37 500 euros which will be used during the f
irst year of the project .

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Nepal Peace Trust Fund
rows.description:
In February 2007 the Government of Nepal established a Nepal Peace Trust Fund in order to ensure sustainable peace in the country. The overall objective of the Fund is to support successful implementation of the Comprehensive Peace Agreement signed between
 the Government of Nepal and the Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) in November 2006. The Peace Trust Fund provides the Government a joint financing mechanism which enables the Government to channel and coordinate resources for the politically agreed key ar
eas. Nepal Peace Trust Fund has had a major role in closing down the military camps of the ex-maoist combatants and in their disarmament and integration.  Providing financial support  to  constitutional assembly elections in November 2013 will be the next 
major challenge for the Peace Trust Fund. Finland has provided 3.0 million euro to the Fund in 2007-2009 and made additional replenishment  of 3.5 million euro in 2010-2013.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: UNDP: Strengthening the Capacity of National Human Rights Commission in Nepal
rows.description:
The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established in May 2000 and was subsequently elevated to the status of a constitutional body by the 2007 Interim Constitution of Nepal. The commission has an extensive mandate to receive complaints conduct in
vestigations and make recommendations but it is lacking in enforcement powers. The UNDP coordinated joint financing project will enhance the capacity of NHRC in the areas of i) auditing human rights compliance issues in legislation policies plans practices
 and procedures which will ensure respect for human rights by state actors ii) ensuring the effective implementation of the international human rights instruments to fulfill state obligations iii) ensuring human rights in the forthcoming constitution and i
v) protecting and promoting the civil political and socio-economic rights. This project will support the NHRC to build both its capacity to work with stakeholders and to meet its accountability and obligations through public opinions inclusive work process
es and reporting. Finnish contribution to the UNDP-led basket fund has been ? 400 000 during 2009-2013. Additional Finnish funding of ? 200 000 is allocated for securing the undisrupted UNDP support to NHRC in view of the upcoming November 2013  parliament
 elections in Nepal and the nomination process of the new Human rights commissioners. Estimated implementation period  2013- 2016.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Institutional Cooperation between Finland and Nepal in meteorological services,  2nd Phase
rows.description:
The Finnish Meteorological Institute has carried out institutional cooperation (ICI) with the Nepalese counterpart Department of Hydrology and Meteorology (DHM) during 2010-2012. The second phase of the ICI-cooepration will be carried out during 2013-2015 
with the budget of nearly 500.000 euro. DHM is an organization under the Ministry of Science Technology and Environment in Nepal. DHM has a mandate from Government to monitor all the hydrological and meteorological activities in Nepal. The scope of work in
cludes the monitoring of river hydrology climate agrometeorology sediment air quality water quality limnology snow hydrology glaciology and wind and solar energy. General and aviation weather forecasts are the regular services provided by DHM. As a member 
of the World Meteorological Organisation (WMO) DHM contributes to the global exchange of meteorological data on a regular basis. The second phase of the Finnish-Nepalese cooperation project will continue to respond to the development needs of DHM. The main
 technology and service areas of DHM development are: data management introducing new advanced technology improvement of weather forecasting (3-day forecasts) and enhancing services for the society. All of these areas need capacity building. The project wi
ll support improvement of the DHM's capacity to plan and develop hydrological and meteorological observation networks data management utilization of hydrometeorological data and provision of hydrometeorological services to the community and the industry. T
his will benefit all citizens of Nepal equally.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: UNDP/Strengthening the Rule of Law and Human Rights Protection System in Nepal
rows.description:
"UNDP has prepared in cooperation with Supreme Court of Nepal Ministry of Law Justice Constituent Assembly and Parliamentary Affairs and National Human Rights Commission a programme titled ""Strengthening the Rule of Law and Human Rights Protection System 
in Nepal"".  The UNDP led program is planned to be an multi-donor program for a five year period. The project basically aims to strengthen the rule of law by supporting firstly the administration of justice sector with the Supreme Court playing a lead role
 through JSCC Secretariat and its network. Secondly through reforms in socio-legal aspect of the rule of law and access to justice sector the project envisages to implement protective laws by developing a national legal aid scheme .Access to justice with f
ocus on vulnerable groups needs special attention and has been positively designed to implement through legal aid centers and legal aid service providers. Affirmative programs with specified figures of the beneficiaries on the other hand have been proposed
 to increase the number of hitherto deprived groups build their competency through educational scholarship internship and apprenticeship. Suggested legislative reforms to reduce discriminatory treatments and opportunities would have both symbolic value as 
well as impelling factor to gear up the project focus towards the needy groups. Total budget estimate for 5 years is USD 24 million of which Finland contributes 58 million euros during 2014-2017."

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Nawalparasi and Palpa Districts Sustainable Water Supply and Sanitation Project (NAPA WASH)
rows.description:
The proposed project NAPA WASH is a continuation of WaterFinns involvement into WASH sector development in Nepal. It aims to complement the past and ongoing water sector support of Finland to Nepal. The Project Purpose is two-fold:1. Improved well-being an
d reduced poverty in Project areas2. Enhanced understanding of long-term sustainability of WASH servicesThe Project aims to  achieve its Purpose through three interlinked results (outputs):Result 1: Lessons learned from success and failure of long-term sus
tainability of rural WASH services reported and benchmarks of best practices promotedResult 2: Capacity building of selected UC?s and VDC?s in their management and efforts of further improving the WASH servicesResult 3: Strengthened institutional structure
 to support communities and UC?s in O+M and Management of WASH services focusing on private sector service provision.One very important element of the proposed project is to create opportunities for the future water sector professionals (both Nepali and Fi
nnish) to have a very interesting possibility to participate to monitoring related work that focuses on long-term learning of the sustainable schemes.The proposed project is three years long (2014-2016) and its proposed budget is 270000 EUR. The project ge
ographic area is Nawalparasi and Palpa Districts.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Education Governance - Promoting Good Governance in the Rural Villages of Lalitpur (EGP)
rows.description:
Although Nepal has made tremendous progress on educational attainment lately the hard to reach group children in the project location villages of Gimdi Asrang and Thuladurlung of Lalitpur district are out of school the drop-out rate is high and the quality
 of education and school management is not satisfactory. Effective management of school and education is seriously problematic highly centralized and with inadequate local ownership.  Besides many community schools have become hotbeds of corruption with lo
cal leaders fighting for the appointment of teachers and resources.  The overall development objective is to attain improved status of education and literacy in project locations through efficient education governance in active engagement of community loca
l government and participation of children. Immediate objectives of the project are enhanced engagement of local governments in education planning and interventions, improved access to school management and quality of education with capacitated accountable
 and democratic School Management Committees (SMC), and increased engagement of parents community and children in educational planning and implementation.The project will enhance the right to quality education for easily marginalized groups such as dalit a
nd tamang (or janajati) children. Inclusive education is promoted to children with disabilities. These actions contribute also to reduction of inequalities. Majority of the target group children are girls and therefore special attention is paid to consider
 gender equality in all activities. Climate sustainability is also taken into account in awareness raising campaigns conducted by child clubs.The project has a comprehensive target group who are key actors on changing school and education environment. It i
ncludes the 4100 target children and their parents policy makers civil society actors media schoolteachers SMCs Parents Teachers Association (PTA) members and education officials. These groups are reached through different interventions, such as capacity b
uilding collaborative consultations with responsible authorities along with wider awareness campaigns and mass mobilizations. The project will be implemented in collaboration of Department of Education Ministry of Federal and Local Governance respective lo
cal governments and District Education Offices and other NGO coalitions active in the sector.

rows.title: People to People Support for Building Community Resilience through Recovery and Reconstruction in Nepal
rows.description: The project aims to minimize the challenges Nepal faces from natural disasters and resultant displacement. Centrally, it builds resilience through sustainable recovery and reconstruction activities in vulnerable communities across eight districts, including by rebuilding critical infrastructure, identifying and mapping open evacuation spaces and training newly elected officials and community members.

rows.title: Capacity building of Nepal's national security forces in camp coordination and camp management (CCCM)
rows.description: Through the present project, IOM aims to contribute to the Government ofNepal's efforts in disaster preparedness through increasing capacity of first responders to effectively manage displaced populations in line with international standards and best practices. A comprehensive response in camp management aims at improving the quality of life and dignity of affected populations during displacement through engagement in participatory processes, working together with, and advocating on behalf of, internally displaced people, as well as other persons affected by humanitarian crisis. The National Red Cross Society (NRCS), as one of the key local responders in Nepal, will also participate in the capacity building activities to bridge the gap between immediate response and longer term management of displaced populations provided byinternational, national and local stakeholders. The proposed action willbe implemented over a two-year period and in close coordination with Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA), Ministry of Defense (MoD), Ministry of Urban Development (MoUD), Department of Urban Development and Building Codes (DUDBC), NA, NP, APF, NRCS and their regional and district offices, and with the support of IOM's Global Camp Coordination and CampManagement (CCCM) Cluster team.

rows.description: The project will contribute to the establishment of the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Authority (NDRRMA) following the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Act 2017, by ensuring that guidelines and by-laws are endorsed for the effective implementation of NDRRMA, focusing on organizational and management structures. The project will achieve this objective through the provision of technical assistance to the Government of Nepal, Ministry of Home Affairs through three strategic outputs.

rows.title: Increasing the Capacity of Migrant Resource Centres in Nepal to Foster Safe, Humane and Orderly Migration
rows.description: The project aims to enhance the capacity of the Government of Nepal by providing potential migrants with accurate and up-to-date information on migration and the recruitment process, so they can make more informed decisions.   Facilitate migration resource centres, which are spread across the various districts of Nepal, in playing a predominant role in migration process by becoming the one-stop-shop for migrants and their families as well as serving to collect data about the migrants for further policy and programming considerations.  The project will also link the migration resource centres by adopting common service level agreements and standard operating procedures in order to provide reliable information to prospective and current migrants through a centralized database.

rows.title: Strengthening Government Capacity in the Development and Implementation of a National Strategic Action Plan on Migration Health in Nepal
rows.description: The main objective of this project is to strengthen the capacity of the Government of Nepal (GoN) to address the health needs of inbound and outbound migrants using a multi-sectoral approach, particularly by reviewing, endorsing and implementing the National Strategic Action Plan(NSAP) on Migration Health. Specifically, over one year the project willsupport and advocate for the Ministry of Health review and endorse the NSAP on migration health, which when implemented will assist in comprehensively addressing health vulnerabilities of migrants and protecting their right to health through a multi-sectorial approach. It will also build the capacity of and support the GoN implement selected activities outlined in the NSAP. With this project 10M will respond to the recommendations from the Regional Dialogue on the Health Challenges for Asian Migrant Workers (July, 2010), the Dhaka Declaration (April, 2011) and the World Health Assembly Resolution 61 .17 (May, 2008) and assist Nepal in implementing these global and regional commitments and comprehensively addressing the multifaceted migration related health challenges.

rows.title: Combating Terrorism Fellowship Program (CTFP)

rows.title: PUBLIC AUDIT REFORM AND CAPACITY BUILDING OF OAGN
rows.description: Objectives of the Grant.    The main objective of the Grant is to help the Office of the Auditor General of Nepal (OAGN) to further enhance the institutional capacity to deliver quality public audit through (a) revising the existing audit guidelines according to the International Organization of Supreme Audit Institutions (INTOSAI) standards, the International Standards on Auditing (ISA) issued by the IFAC,  and the changes in Government rules and requirements, in the following public audit areas  Revenue Audit, Procurement/ Public Works Audit, Project Audit and  General Public Audit, (b)  revising and consolidating the audit standards and guidelines, (c)  improving the Auditor Generals reporting methodology commensurate with best international practices, and  (d) developing the Human Resource Development Plan including the strengthening of its Training Division to create a strong and fully equipped training unit capable of providing quality training to its staff.    To meet this objective, the grant will help review the existing guidelines and develop new guidelines in the areas mentioned above, and update audit standards and guidelines.  In addition, it will help establish institutionalized training arrangements to implement the guidelines within OAGN, and conduct training programs.  The grant will also help to provide exposure to senior officers through carefully planned, monitored, and evaluated programs of short term training secondment and training exchanges with well-developed Supreme Audit Institutes (SAIs) in South or East Asia or Pacific countries, and  twinning with another successful SAI.   These senior officers will be developed as Trainers who will then impart training to auditors responsible for the management of public audit. Background.    The Nepal Country Assistance Strategy (CAS dated November 17, 1998) identified governance, management of public expenditures and institutional constraints as serious impediments preventing Nepal from achieving its full economic potential and reducing poverty. In its Executive Summary, it specifies two new prongs:  (a) bringing resources closer to the beneficiaries,  where they are most likely to be productively used, and (b) fostering the strong governance needed to reduce waste and mismanagement.    Good governance, public accountability and corruption control are stated to be high priorities of the Government.  The Auditor General promotes and upholds the public accountability, credibility and discipline in financial operations.  Quality audit helps to identify measures to eliminate or reduce waste and inefficiency in the mobilisation of public resources, to create public awareness on good governance, to recommend the improvement of policies and procedures, and to  sharpen the process of public accountability. Emerging changes in technology and growing demands from the public and donors for public accountability and good governance are putting pressure on the OAGN to be more proactive, quality conscious and oriented to serving the public interest.  The technical assistance provided to date through the Banks past IDF grants  has greatly contributed  to the institutional development of OAGN. The first IDF grant helped to set up a stage for  institutionalising the audit procedures and practices.  The project contributed the five manuals which was immediately put in use, although they were implemented without field testing.   A few policy decisions were made during the tenure of the project making departure from conservative approach to a more demand approach.   The first IDF was rated unsatisfactory during exit.    The second IDF grant was a follow on project which was deemed essential to carry on with the activities initiated during the first IDF, to continue to build the momentum, and to appreciate the Banks support to whatever marginal achievements were attempted.  The project continued to focus in improving auditing of development projects and public enterprises , and provide training to staff.  Performance improved as compared to the first IDF.  Outcomes achieved during the second IDF are becoming sustainable.  It was rated Partially Satisfactory at exit.   The third IDF which was provided to help OAG build a performance auditing capability as its contribution to improving the financial management of public sector projects was rated Highly Satisfactory.  A strong commitment demonstrated by the Auditor General and ownership taken by him enabled to fully achieve the development objectives.   Performance Audit Manual was developed, staff were trained, twinning arrangement with Mala ysian OAG was arranged, and performance auditing capacity has been internalised.     In recognition of the fact that capacity building is an ongoing effort, it is important for the Bank to continue to be the development partner of OAGN to support its institutional development efforts.   An added advantage is that the same Auditor General who implemented the third IDF is committed to continue reforms in auditing and improving the capability of his office. The recently completed Country Financial Accountability Assessment (CFAA dated July 5, 2002) jointly carried out by His Majestys Government of Nepal (HMGN) and the World Bank confirmed that HMGN is serious and committed to improving public and private sector financial management.  It has concluded that reforms in financial management are critical and should be approached in an integrated way covering planning, budgeting, accounting, internal control, internal audit, external audit and oversight.  With regard to public sector audit, the CFAA has recognized the strong and dedicated leadership of the OAGN,  and has also concluded that the OAGN sets a good example of fearless reporting.  A strong partnership forged by OAGN with the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) to follow-up on irregularities and other accountability issues is an impressive example,  as confirmed by the CFAA.  While recognizing the OAGNs strong points, the CFAA has made several recommendations to further enhance its capacity.    HMGN has endorsed the recommendations of the CFAA and has committed to implement the Development Action Plan proposed  therein.  Recommendations related to public audit have been discussed and agreed with HMGN and OAGN.  The CFAA recommended  actions which include, inter alia, increasing OAGNs independence,  providing sufficient resources to OAGN to cope with the needs for more training, equipment, and expertise,  reviewing and revising audit standards and guidelines according to INTOSAI standards and to accommodate changes in HMGN rules, developing Human Resource Development Plan including the strengthening of its training division to create a strong and fully equipped training unit capable of providing quality training to its staff, and providing exposure to senior officials of OAGN through carefully planned, monitored, and evaluated programs of short-term training secondment and training exchanges with well-developed SAIs abroad. The Auditor General requested the Banks IDF grant about a year back to support OAGN with its institutional development efforts.  A draft proposal was submitted to the Bank, which was reviewed and discussed with OAGN.  The Bank suggested that the OAGN should postpone this request until the CFAA was completed, and indicated that the Bank would be interested in providing support to the OAGN in line with the CFAA recommendations.  The previous draft has been slightly modified along the lines of the CFAA recommendations, and this request is to support the specific action plans of CFAA to improve the quality of public audit.   A few actions related to OAGN are already in the process of implementation as a result of strong commitment of the Auditor General.  For example, the long standing issue of repealing the Auditors Act 1974 to stop the licensing of auditors by the OAGN, and to pass this responsibility to the Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nepal (ICAN),  has recently been resolved after the endorsement by the Cabinet of the Auditor Generals proposal.  The Auditor General has also agreed to arrange for an external peer review of, and report on, its work at least once every two years by a reputable private audit firm.  He has also agreed to have the OAGN accounts audited independently each year by a reputable private audit firm.  Of the six specific CFAA recommendations to be implemented by the OAGN, two have already been implemented, a third related to the decentralization of the audit function by establishing OAGN field offices is under discussion, and the remaining three related to capacity building are expected to be implemented through the proposed IDF grant. All INTOSAI members, including the OAG of Nepal, are expected to apply INTOSAI auditing standards.  International Standards on Auditing (ISAs) issued by IFAC are very similar to INTOSAI standards, and efforts have been going on for some years to harmonize them as much as possible.  The grant will be used to:  (i) ensure that all OAGN auditing standards and guidelines comply with INTOSAI standards; (ii) review ISAs to identify any aspects representing an improvement on INTOSAI standards; and (iii) introduce any such improvement into OAG standards and guidelines. The Activities to be Financed Under the Grant.    HMGN has requested the Bank (its letter of  August 14, 2002) to provide technical assistance to upgrade the quality of audit through a grant to assist OAGN to: wReview and revise audit guidelines according to INTOSAI standards; wReview the existing reporting procedure and format of the Auditor General Annual Report, and recommend improvements in the AGs reporting, aiming to downsize the reporting volume; and wPrepare a Human Resource Development Plan,  including the strengthening of OAGNs Training Division to create a strong and fully Based on the recommendations of the CFAA and the agreement reached with the OAGN, the following activities will be financed under the grant, and carried out by international and national consultants under the guidance of a steering committee to be formed in the OAGN: wPrepare a time-bound work plan to implement the activities of the grant.  Include in the plan arrangements for in-house discussion series and inter-departmental seminars and discussions meetings in all stages of preparation of audit guidelines. wReview existing functions, policies, strategies, procedures, audit methodology, audit guidelines for revenue, procurement/public works, project accounts, general public sector audit standards, and staffing of OAGN,  in accordance with international best practices such as the INTOSAI standards, the International Standards on Auditing (ISA) issued by the IFAC,  and establish benchmarks on all these matters, in order to introduce appropriate reforms within OAGN. wRevise the existing audit guidelines according to INTOSAI standards, or the ISA (and also taking into consideration the changes in Government rules and requirements), in the following public sector audit areas  Revenue Audit, Procurement/ Public Works Audit, Project Audit and  other Public Sector Audit Procedures. wPilot the revised guidelines in a few selected development projects before official adoption.  Supervise the OAGN auditors in conducting the pilot audit to apply the revised guidelines.  Finalize the audit guidelines both in English and in Nepali and officially adopt these guidelines. reporting, aiming to downsize the reporting volume. wPrepare a Human Resource Development Plan including the strengthening of OAGNs Training Division to create a strong and fully equipped training unit capable of providing quality training to its staff and capable of maintaining a good Training database. Provide exposure  The purpose of the study tours is to provide exposure to senior officials to a well-developed SAI on how the audit guidelines are prepared , how the training is conducted to staff, and how to present a succinct audit report; it will also provide exposure and skills on developing them as the Trainers who will take the responsibility to train other staff in OAG; the consulting firm will take responsibility to arrange the study tour program. to a selected group of  senior officials Officials will include both senior and junior level officials having a combination of first class, second class and third class gazetted officials; a few of them will be developed as a trainer of OAGN (about 135 staff  Number of officials proposed is 13, but this figure may decrease or increase depending on the country which will be identified for the study tour as per the proposal of the consulting firm, within the limitation of the total budget allocated in the proposal.) through carefully planned, monitored, and evaluated programs of short-term training secondment and training exchanges in the above mentioned areas with well-developed SAIs in South or East Pacific countries and twinning with a successful SAI  The bidder will be asked to indicate in the proposal the name of the SAI with which twinning arrangement will be made. (also, assist the Steering Committee to develop transparent selection criteria to select candidates for exposure visit). These officials will be developed as Trainers who will be experts in developing annual training plan and imparting training to other audit staff. wDevelop a training toolkit (which should include audit methodology, reporting, quality control aspects etc.) in each audit area and train OAGN staff through formal class-room training and through on-the-job training in pilot audits, aiming to develop a network of expertise in each audit area. wUpgrade the computer skills of OAGN staff by providing training in Microsoft application programs (which include Word, Excel and Access). wPrepare a project completion report providing details of work performed during the assignment correlating with each of the benchmarks, problems encountered and resolved and recommendations/proposals for ongoing efforts of OAGN in sustainable institutional development.   Also, include a supplementary feedback of the Steering Committee on the report on behalf of OAGN. In order to ensure the quality of audit guidelines and training toolkit and overall assessment of the work delivery under the program, the Supreme Audit Institution with which a twinning arrangement will be made will also be requested to send a senior official from the SAI to visit  OAG Nepal towards the end of the program as part of the twinning, to review all the works carried out and provide a formal peer review as part of independent assessment on the quality of OAG work.  Costs of the visit of the SAI official is built in as part of the international consultants visit; this requirement will be spelled out in the Request for Proposal. Likely Outcome of the Grant. The proposed activities to be financed under the grant will create the enabling environment to produce internationally recognized public sector auditing standards, and a better quality staff through a self-sustained staff training capacity within OAGN.  All these are expected to result in a better quality audit by enhancing the oversight capacity of OAGN.  This should also help to contain corruption and improve the efficiency with which public expenditures are managed.    ,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,, Relationship of Grant Proposal with Country Economic and Sector Work (or Business Plan)    As stated above,  the Nepal Country Assistance Strategy (CAS dated November 17, 1998) identified governance, public expenditure management, corruption  and overall institutional constraints as serious impediments preventing Nepal from achieving its full economic potential and reducing poverty.  Actions identified in the CAS to move to a base case lending scenario, inter alia, included reducing delays in decision-making, improving program implementation and adopting remedial measures to control misuse of funds and corruption.  Upgrading the audit quality by revising the audit guidelines according to INTOSAI standards or the ISA and international best practices, preparing and implementing a Human Resource Development Plan in OAGN, and improving the skills of audit staff,  would contribute to the realization of the above-mentioned CAS objectives.    Also, as explained in the last two paragraphs of the Background section above, the activities proposed in this document for IDF grant financing are part of the CFAA Development Action Plan. Government Agency Responsible for Execution    Office of the Auditor General of Nepal (OAGN) Government Contribution (Cash or In-Kind)    US$ 58,000 in Kind in form of staff costs and office space and in cash to cover required operating costs. Other Comments If Applicable    None. Experience with the Implementation of Previous IDF Grants.    OAGN has the experience of implementing three IDF Grants.   The third IDF Grant was highly successful in terms of meeting the development objectives and accomplishing all targeted activities, as a result of experience gained through the implementation of the first two IDF grants which were not so successful.   A high degree of ownership and commitment by OAGN in program implementation of the third IDF is a major factor leading to success.   Based on previous experience, and with a strong and dedicated leadership of the Auditor General, implementation of the proposed grant is expected to be fully owned and managed by OAGN in accordance with its institutional needs and with a determination to continue to improve the audit quality by means of revisions in guidelines and by upgrading the skills of staff through sustainable and institutionalised training programs.  In the context of the recent Nepal Development Forum and the CFAA discussions, the Government and OAGN reaffirmed their commitment to moving ahead for instituting good governance and improved financial accountability.  In view of the many strong points noted above, the risk of not attaining the set objectives is very low. The Grant will be executed by the Office of the Auditor General of Nepal, with support from a qualified accounting/audit consulting firm, staffed by at least three international consultants, and about four national consultants for various purposes of the activities.  The team of consultants should include tax auditors, public audit expert, management consultant, information technology expert and training expert.  A high level Steering Committee will be formed under the chairpersonship of one of the Deputy Auditor Generals.  The Committee will also include, inter alia, representatives from professional bodies.  It will also invite selected donor representatives as observers.   The Committee will provide direction in the program implementation, and will also monitor the program implementation.  The Committee will monitor the performance of the consultants.  The Committee will periodically brief the Auditor General, and will obtain his overall guidance in program implementation.  The Auditor General will nominate the members of the Steering Committee which will include officials from OAGN, independent experts and professionals, and representatives from a few auditees.  The Auditor General will also appoint a Project Coordinator who will serve the SC as the Member Secretary.  The Project Coordinator will be responsible for overall project administration, reporting to the SC, and to the World Bank, and maintain all records and accounts of the grant.

rows.title: PUBLIC PROCUREMENT REFORM IN NEPAL
rows.description: The main objective of the Grant is to help His Majestys Government of Nepal (HMGN) to enhance the quality of public procurement through procedural and institutional reforms, including adoption of a comprehensive procurement law, revision and consolidation of all existing procurement rules and regulations, preparation of a framework for institutionalizing procurement training, and implementation of training program. To meet this objective, the grant will help develop a draft modern transparent and competitive Public Procurement Law, and related rules and procedures.  In addition, it will help establish institutionalized training arrangements and conduct initial training program, particularly for trainers and officials responsible for the management of public procurement including contract administration.

rows.title: Improve Nepal's Public through Pension Reform and Strengthening Capacity to Implement Reform
rows.description: The grant will support technical assistance and training to support the following objectives: (a)   Reform of the Civil Pension Scheme including the introduction of a contributory scheme through support for (i) actuarial analysis, (ii) participatory dialogue to define and develop reform proposals, (iii) preparing rules and regulations for the reformed scheme and (iv) implementation of the reformed scheme. (b)   Reform of the Employee Provident Fund Scheme(EPF) through support for (i) financial and actuarial analysis to develop reforms and (ii) implementation of the reforms. (c)   Increase the effectiveness of the Universal Old Age Pension Scheme through support for (i) assessing the effectiveness of the current scheme to develop any necessary proposals for improvement and (ii) implementation of any reforms. The original objectives of the Grant were to develop capacity for the GoN to reform and improve its existing pension system which includes the civil service pension scheme, the Employee Provident Fund and the Old Age Assistance scheme.  The capacity to be developed would include both policy and implementation.

rows.title: Legal and Judicial reforms to Strengthen Creditor Rights
rows.description: The proposed grant has two components - (i) Strengthening judicial delivery in commercial and banking cases by establishing a new commercial court, and (ii) obtaining baseleine data on Judiciary.  (i) Nepal has recently passed a set of legislations (such as the Insolvency Ordinance) vesting jurisdiction in a yet to be established Commercial Court. These matters are presently being taken to the general courts, whose functioning leaves a lot to be desired. In absence of continuing training being provided to judges (see below), judges get insufficient exposure to commercial practices. There is no awareness of the opportunity cost when productive assets get blocked in the courts. An all new Commercial Court looks an attractive alternative, but may possibly be unproductive as an independent entity. Another way of achieving this may be through a dedicated Commercial Bench at the Appellate and Supreme Court level. This sub-component of the grant will finance the (i) setting up of the Commercial Bench, the procedures, manual, case management system, (ii) an assessment of training needs and development of modules which can be used to train the judges and support staff. (iii) study of existing model of division of jurisdictions and performance of institutions. (ii) Lengthy court proceedings are perhaps the most visible symptom of the crisis in the Nepali judiciary. They translate into significant and perpetual backlogs - a breeding ground for petty corruption. There have been many initiatives to tackle this problem.  Most of them have focused at case management in the narrow sense of processing papers and reorganizing and/or automating some of the business operations. More is needed to sustain increase court productivity and reduce both time and costs of court operations.  Such reforms should certainly include consolidation of court jurisdiction and competence so the cases are decided with less cost without compromising quality.  The reform should expand to include the distribution of cases among the courts to allocation of cases to judges.  Judicial Map (geographical organization of the courts) seems also to contribute to lower performance of the courts, as large number of the courts necessarily impede specialization and lead to difficult and costly supervision. Although civil procedures in Nepal does not seem to be terribly formal (28 steps comparing to 29.7 regional average) they are in need of improvement.  Endless opportunities for appeals seem to be a big problem which, in addition to increasing delay and costs, raises opportunities for corruption.  To formulate the foregoing reforms it is necessary to have the necessary data and information on the judiciary. This subcomponent of the grant will finance the -(i) the collection of baseline data, including public perception of judiciary (ii) judicial mapping (iii) assessment of judges performance The objective of the Project is to build capacity of the judiciary in providing dispute resolution services in banking and commercial matters through commercial bench and data collection and analysis.

rows.title: Strengthening Institutional Capacity of the Public Procurement Monitoring Office
rows.description: Institutional Strengthening of the Public Procurement Monitoring Office (PPMO) to support the implementation of the recently passed Nepal Public Procurement Act 2007. Development Objective To promote good governance and public service delivery through supporting public procurement reforms to enhance the quality of procurement outcomes in terms of: higher volumes, efficiency, effectiveness, transparency and better value for money. Link to CAS and/or ESW (core diagnostics, e.g., CPAR, CFAA, PER, CEM, etc.), and relationship to Bank operations : According to the 2004 CAS, Nepal will have to undertake a number of governance reforms in order to stimulate a more pro-poor and inclusive growth process. The CAS stresses the significance of capacity building efforts in improving governance, and the role of efficient public procurement in the economy.  As recommended by the CPAR 2002, the Government of Nepal enacted a Public Procurement Law in 2007 along with Public Procurement Regulations, 2007. It is essential that these laws and regulations be fully implemented since the most recent assessment of the Nepal Public Financial Management System, the Public Financial Management Sector Work recently carried out jointly by the Government of Nepal and the World Bank, found once again that procurement performance remains a very significant issues and achieved a low rating (C) for value of money and controls in procurement.  The Government has a huge challenge to ensure effective implementation of the Law, and the Bank needs to continue its support and engagement in this sector. Work to implement the Procurement Law is completely in concert with the governance initiative as envisioned in the updated CAS 2007. To assist Government in preparing a strategy for and initiating actions to implement the public procurement law so as to enhance the quality of procurement outcomes in terms of: higher volumes, efficiency, optimum budget utilisation, effectiveness, transparency and better value for money which would lead to better governance and improved public service delivery.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Community Leadership on Education and Protection (CLEAP)
rows.description:
The Community Leadership on Education and Protection (CLEAP) project strengthens local child rights protection and education systems and school management, empowering parents and children to claim their right to education and protection in Sudurpaschim (Far West) province of Nepal. The project addresses problem like school dropouts and discrimination in communities, strengthening civil society engagement for promoting accountability and good governance in schools and local governments.

 

 Gender-, caste- and disability-based discrimination are still deeply rooted and widely accepted in Far West Nepal. Dalit communities and children with disabilities are discriminated against in private and public spheres. Vulnerable girls face multiple discriminatory practices including early marriages, high responsibility of household chores, son preference, domestic violence and being considered impure during menstruation.

 

 Considering learning from past projects and feedback from community, officials and government authorities, project aims at strengthening right to education and protection for vulnerable children with three major results:

 

 1) Strengthened and functional child protection systems and education management

 2) Children, parents, media, and civil society actors are actively engaged to promote right to education and protection for vulnerable children

 3) Improved Right to Education through increased enrollment, reduced drop out, improved learning outcomes and inclusion 

 

 The project reaches about 2 000 vulnerable children (girls, Dalits and children with disabilities) and parents, civil society actors and 125 public and elected officials at local and provincial levels. The direct beneficiaries are children, parents and teachers (72) of four wards in Nawadurga and Bhageshwor Rural Municipalities of Dadeldhura District. In addition, stakeholders include civil society activists as well as media (40+).

 

 The project is jointly implemented by child rights expert organizations Interpedia and Loo Niva Child Concern Group.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Nepal office PMER support
rows.description: 1. Project: 10837 FCA Nepal Country Office (NEPCO) 2. Project area and Country: Nepal 3. Project justification:  FCA Nepal country programme continues promoting development cooperation after completing three years (2015-2017) of humanitarian assistance after the earthquake. FCA will continue to provide Technical Vocational Education and Training (TVET) services and effectively link learning to earning, and develop and strengthen village level saving and credits cooperatives to enhance access to finance. FCA will stress on youth mobilization in awareness of development issues and combating caste based discrimination, and building capacity of CBOs in leadership development, organizational management and advocacy towards the local government. FCA?s field presence is essential for increased impact and enhanced programme quality. It is in line with FCA?s strategy and supports FCA?s strategic goals related to programme development and quality, capacity building, advocacy, thematic/technical support, monitoring and diversification of the funding base. 4. The main objective and expected results: The objective is to improve effectiveness, quality and impact of FCA?s Nepal country programme through established field presence.  The expected results are:  - Established a functional working relationship and collaboration with UN agencies, I/NGOs, CBOs and government authorities at district and national level  - New partnership and networking opportunities are created to broadening the funding opportunities   - Effective coordination of work and cooperation with other aid organizations  - Capacity of local partners are built for effective and efficient programme implementation  - Strategic and operational planning for the country programme including programme and project PME and administrative support  5. Right-holders/ beneficiaries: Nepal Country Office staff and partners 6. Implementing partner: FCA Nepal Country Office

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Improving school environment in Nepal
rows.description: In Nepal, Taksvärkki, in cooperation with its partner Environmental Camps for Conservation Awareness, ECCA (https://ecca.org.np/home), supports student council activities and strengthens cooperation between student councils and other school actors to improve the learning environment. The aim is to increase young people's opportunities to participate in the development of their own school environment and communities. Through the training, young people learn important skills needed in student council activities and advocacy, as well as knowledge of the importance of clean water, hygiene and the environment for well-being. At the same time, the project will disseminate the knowledge and good practices related to the environment, health and human rights they have learned more widely in their communities. Children and young people in rural village schools in Nepal face a number of challenges. Not all parents see the importance of education. Especially girls' schooling may be considered a waste of time. The lack of clean drinking water, water supply and sanitation in schools contributes to the spread of infectious diseases and reduces the attractiveness of schools. In addition to child marriages, the lack of decent and separate toilets for girls is a major cause of girls dropping out of school. Discrimination based on gender and social status is also a problem. Due to the remoteness, school authorities may not have contact with village schools, so their reality is not taken into account in decision-making in the education sector. The project enhances the study conditions in village schools by supporting student council activities and strengthening co-operation between student councils, parents, teacher associations, education authorities and other key actors. The learning environment in the village schools will be improved, including decent facilities for study, clean drinking water, clean green environment, and hygienic toilets. Student council activities strengthen the participation of children and youth in schools and create conditions for effective co-operation between different actors. In addition to decent toilets, girls? right to education will be promoted by increasing girls' awareness and ability to take care of menstrual hygiene. The schooling of low-income children and youth, especially girls, the dalits and other vulnerable pupils, will be supported, and thus, the project promotes the principle of equality in project schools.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Rural Village Water Resources Management Project (III phase)
rows.description: The Rural Village Water Resources Management Project in Nepal, Phase II (2010-2015), has after four years of implementation achieved better water supply for 93 000 people, sanitation for 269 000 people and home gardens for 155 000 people. The purpose of phase III is to achieve full coverage of water supply and sanitation, and establishment of functional planning and implementation frameworks for all water uses in nine project districts. The expected results of the project are: 1) institutionalized community capacity to construct and maintain community managed water supply and adopt appropriate technologies and behavior 2) Improved and sustainable nutrition, food security and sustainable income at community level through water resources based livelihoods development 3) Institutionalized capacity at district level to continue integrated water resources planning and support to communities in implementing and maintaining WASH and livelihood activities. In terms of beneficiaries, the project will reach over 300 000 people. The executing agency is Ministry of Federal Affairs and Local Development of Nepal together with participating District Development Committees (DDC). Finland will support the project with 15 million euros from 2015 to 2020.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Support to Nepal's School Sector Development Plan (SSDP), Covid-19 response
rows.description:
In spring 2020, the Nepal Ministry of Education and the Nepal Education Cluster have jointly developed a separate COVID-19 adaptation plan (COVID-19 Education Cluster Contingency Plan - Nepal Education Cluster, 2020) for the education sector programme SSDP (The School Sector Development Plan). The Ministry of Education made a general request for all donors, including Finland, of additional funding for covid-19 emergency response.

 

 The overall objective of the Education Cluster Contingency Plan is to prevent the spread of COVID-19 from education institutions into local communities by providing safe learning environments by putting in place appropriate prevention measures in Nepalese schools. To achieve this objective the immediate response of the Education Cluster will focus on a) Ensuring adequate capacity for management and coordination during the response period, b) Strengthening prevention and resilience within the school system and among students, teachers, parents and caregivers and c) Support Continuity of Education/Learning for all children in all areas including children with disabilities and from marginalized backgrounds.

 

 Finland's financial contrbution for the implementation of the Education Cluster Contingency Plan for COVID-19 is EUR 2 514 999. The support will be channeled and monitored as part of Nepal School Sector Development Plan (SSDP) programme. The School Sector Development Plan (SSDP) program covers the entire basic education system in Nepal and is funded jointly by the Government of Nepal and its Development Partners. The program is implemented during the years 2016-2023. Finland supports the program with a total of 20 million euros in the years 2016-2020.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Planning funds for Finland's Embassy in Kathmandu
rows.description:
Implementing development cooperation in Nepal requires the identification and preparation of new projects as well as managing, monitoring and evaluating ongoing projects. An important part of the Embassy's work is also cooperation and coordination between donors. In addition, the successful management of development cooperation funds requires the staff to regularly participate in trainings relating to Finnish development cooperation and policy.  

 

 Planning funds are annually made available to the Embassy of Finland in Kathmandu to address the above mentioned needs. The funds may thus be used for preparing, planning, monitoring and evaluating development cooperation projects and programs as well as reviews and studies related to future funding. In addition, training needs of the staff may also be covered from these funds. 

 

 Funding for the year 2019 is 40 000 Euros.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Institutional Cooperation between Finland and Nepal in meteorological services;  3rd Phase
rows.description:
The Finnish Meteorological Institute has carried out institutional cooperation (ICI) with the Nepalese counterpart Department of Hydrology and Meteorology (DHM) in the years 2010-2012 and 2013-2017. DHM is an organization under the Ministry of Population and Environment in Nepal. DHM has a mandate from Government to monitor all the hydrological and meteorological activities in Nepal.

 

 The first phase of the project was evaluated in 2012. The conclusion was that DHM had a high ownership of the project and mutual accountability had been evident in the project. It was noted, that the project had addressed the specific needs of the DHM and the impact of the project had been positive. However improvements were suggested to wider dissemination of skills within the Department, results-based management and targeting of participants to the trainings. The recommendations were taken into account in the planning of the second phase of the project.

 

 The third phase of the project will continue to respond to the development needs of the Government of Nepal and the DHM, in the years 2018-2021. The funding for the third phase is 500 000 euros. The overall objective of the project is improved capability of the Government of Nepal to respond to the increased risks of natural disasters related to weather and climate. The project has three main expected results: 1) Improved processes of DHM to manage the new observation systems 2) Improved production and service system 3) Enhanced Customer Relations

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Strengthening livelihoods, civil society and community-based natural resource management of indigenous communities
rows.description:
Chepangs and Tamangs live in the hills and mountains of central Nepal facing many types of hazard that affect both lives and livelihood.They are regarded as the most marginalized and resource poor groups in Nepal.Their tiny land holdings provide no more than 3 to 6 months of food security.The forest and overall landscape is degrading because of the human pressure in forest land.The already formed Community Forest User Groups are not functioning well because of their limited capacity.Due to lack of knowledge of efficient and sustainable agricultural practices and land-tenure rights, the communities practicing the traditional slash and burn cultivation have reduced the cultivation cycle from 3-5 years to one year.This had led to serious soil erosion problems that cause regular landslides that destroy agricultural fields and threaten the lives of people and animals.

 

 The main objective of this project is to uplift the livelihoods and food security of the Chepang and Tamang communities through agro-forestry development and improved community-based natural resource management.This is achieved by developing sound biodiversity management and sustainable livelihood activities in order to maintain, rebuild, and revitalize socio-ecological production landscape.Capacity building of CFUGs will be done to strenghten their operational capacity and women's self-help groups will be formed to promote women's empowerment and livelihood.The project will build capacities of local civil society organizations and duty-brearers and promote positive interaction between local government and Chepang and Tamang communities.

 

 The project implemented by NAFAN and supported by the Swallows supports Finland's development objectives by increasing food security and promoting sustainable use of natural resources, local democracy and participation of women in decision making. The project will strengthen local organization's capacity in community capacity building and establishing linkages between communities and local government. The project is expected to benefit directly around 2200 right-holders and 100 duty-bearers. In addition to this, approx. 3000 people are expected to benefit indirectly.
